Bear case for crypto: the same collateral is used to trade stocks instead of coins

Bull: a lot more stables are onboarded for this activity and they find their way into coins

iho, the bull case is mostly bullish for the use case that onboards rather than majors

Long perps

tweets.text: @robin_j_brooks Why does the size of the fed balance sheet have anything to do with the public debt?  The deficit sends money to the private sector and sources money from the private sector.  The Fed is not a necessary participant.

thread_tweets.text: @robin_j_brooks Why does the size of the fed balance sheet have anything to do with the public debt?  The deficit sends money to the private sector and sources money from the private sector.  The Fed is not a necessary participant.

thread_tweets.text:
This is true.  

I am absolutely FOR balance sheet decline and composition shift to less long duration assets and no mortgages.  Have been since QT hadn't even been announced.  However the OP somehow linked balance sheet growth with debt growth and how it's impossible to reduce the balance sheet while deficits are 7%.  That's neither a correct linkage nor even factually true as over two year the balance sheet has shrunk by 6% of GDP as deficits have stayed at 6%+   Also the economy has stayed hot despite the withdrawl of balance sheet.   The balance sheet can shrink and be less MBS and Bonds while the deficit stays high and the economy stays fine.
thread_tweets.full_text:
This is true.  

I am absolutely FOR balance sheet decline and composition shift to less long duration assets and no mortgages.  Have been since QT hadn't even been announced.  However the OP somehow linked balance sheet growth with debt growth and how it's impossible to reduce the balance sheet while deficits are 7%.  That's neither a correct linkage nor even factually true as over two year the balance sheet has shrunk by 6% of GDP as deficits have stayed at 6%+   Also the economy has stayed hot despite the withdrawl of balance sheet.   The balance sheet can shrink and be less MBS and Bonds while the deficit stays high and the economy stays fine.

thread_tweets.text:
The only buyer is the private sector and though the "recipient" may not and probably won't buy the debt.  But he will buy something and the recipient including a foreigner if he sells an export will ultimately have no other choice but to buy the treasury debt.  

It is a tautology that the deficit sends money to the private sector and absent the Fed the private sector buys the debt.
thread_tweets.full_text:

It is a tautology that the deficit sends money to the private sector and absent the Fed the private sector buys the debt.

thread_tweets.text:
Yes.  If we are talking about the Fed needing to increase the balance sheet because they need to artificially suppress rates and provide inflationary and real growth stimulus that's a different question.  The Fed and the Treasury have constantly been unwilling to allow interest rates to clear at a market price. 

BUT there is no mechanical reason why the Fed needs to participate in the market EXCEPT for monetary policy reasons

tweets.text: Warren was not just an investor.  I dealt with him on single name overrights for 90 day calls, index puts, and merger arbitrage.  My colleagues dealt with him in high yield, govies, and rates derivatives .  He was as sharp a trader as anyone I have seen. Shark!  And a tuna sandwich for lunch.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Warren Buffet decided to get a tuna fish sandwich and I missed an opportunity to take him for 10 million dollars.

Back in 1989 i became the first and only market maker in a stub equity warrant given to holders of the junk bonds KKR used to finance the LBO of RJ Reynolds.

Wagmi is a feel good phrase but highly inaccurate. To make it you need 3 things. Most will fail at least one of these and not make it:

1) 24/7 work that pushes you to sacrifice time with family, friends etc.
2) Right market at right time
3) High conviction. Buy blood, sell fomo.

tweets.text:
A reminder for those who burn our flag and say that America is a terrible country…

If your world view was correct, millions of people would be fleeing this great republic today, including you. But in reality, tens of millions of people from all around the world are fleeing their home nations for the slightest chance of coming here.

This is it.
America is the shining city on the hill.

It’s not our job to let in the entire world. It’s our job to make sure America remains the shining city for our children and grandchildren.

Happy 4th. 🇺🇸🤝

tweets.text:
Bruh, when I first tried out ChatGPT around 2023, I thought it LLMs were garbage at coding. 

3Y later, Mythos is considered a modern day “nuke” for cybersecurity. 

People keep saying humanoids can’t do X or Y task today like plumbing or DC wiring. No sht, but it’s about where things are heading over the few years.

And it’s clear to me and VC apparently that we’ve just hit the inflection point where soon: majority of labor you think is human-only, can be replaced by robotics/humanoids. 

You're already seeing this too with internal strategies: Remember $AMZN leaked strat planned to avoid hiring 600,000 workers by them with robots like $CCXI? 

If you see past the "assistive robotics" public image framing to prevent protest, it's clear the goals is maximizing, opex efficiency. 

The main areas I think are safe however are regulation bound (eg. Medicine), ultra specialized labor, or require human/emotional connections.

But I think you'll be blown away by the rate of change when frontier technologies become national security races between US and China... especially when China is currently in the lead.

tweets.text:
There’s a lot of focus on direct vault ratings

But for our risk data I decided to go the other way and show all the ways users could lose money, the risks they take

Our goal is not to make risks decisions for users but help them do that and cover their blind spots on any risks they missed
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
When you deposit into a vault, you're exposed to more than the vault itself: oracles, bridges, collateral, the sequencer, holders of vault roles.

Introducing risk scenario planning on DefiLlama.

* Maps every dependency that touches your deposit
* Models your max loss from each https://t.co/eeJPJOpW8e
tweets.full_text:
There’s a lot of focus on direct vault ratings

But for our risk data I decided to go the other way and show all the ways users could lose money, the risks they take

Our goal is not to make risks decisions for users but help them do that and cover their blind spots on any risks they missed

a remarkable period

tweets.text:
Thanks for all the feedback on the last post. 

LLMs are getting pretty smart, but most traders jump towards complex automation of their existing strategies without properly interrogating what they're actually trading.

Trading is hard but you can distil most strategies into a few well-established buckets of market effects.

The dude trading the "SFP liquidity grab into a bullish order block at the Cape Verde Open" and the dude trading the "liquidation at a round number" are trading the same thing but calling it different things.

The valuable part is in the mechanism, not in the label.

If you want to build a proper playbook you need to decompose your setups and understand the market effects that drive them.

I mentioned that you can do this with an LLM but didn't specify how.

Just paste this into your LLM of choice:

You're an educational trading companion. Your job is to lift the fog on what I actually trade.

Interview me one question at a time. If I can name a setup I trade, start there. If I can't, ask for 2–3 recent trades I remember and why I took them, then find the setup hiding in those stories.

Decompose every setup from folklore into first principles:

• the real, well-studied market effect I'm exploiting (momentum/imbalance, forced flow, trend, mean reversion, herding, positioning — not exhaustive; e.g. a "triangle breakout" is really a balance-to-imbalance shift betting on aggressive taker flow)
• the mechanism: who's forced to act, why the flow exists
• when the effect is active vs dormant
• which of my confluence factors follow from the mechanism, and which are folklore
• invalidation that follows from the mechanism

Be a companion, not an examiner. Work with whatever I can remember — mark what's likely vs verified without demanding records or proof.

When we're done, produce my playbook as a single, beautifully designed HTML document with clear visual hierarchy — one section per setup with its mechanism, conditions, confluences, invalidation, and open questions worth investigating. If I paste in an existing playbook, refine it — don't start over.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
As someone who loves trading technicals

I think learning about markets via technicals (like I did) is one of the worst ways to start

It’s a rigid framework where grown men argue with each other about the exact Japanese name for a specific candlestick or a box they’ve drawn on

thread_tweets.text:
Generally started seeing better results with 1) wider invalidations; 2) giving the trade more time if it's MTF-HTF; 3) shoving below support/above resistance; 4) not engaging in scaling or too much partial chicanery, at most a couple of clips

The ridigity layer should be in the setup side vs trigger side IMO (which makes sense because that's where most of the edge is supposed to be)

tweets.full_text:
Thanks for all the feedback on the last post. 

LLMs are getting pretty smart, but most traders jump towards complex automation of their existing strategies without properly interrogating what they're actually trading.

Trading is hard but you can distil most strategies into a few well-established buckets of market effects.

The dude trading the "SFP liquidity grab into a bullish order block at the Cape Verde Open" and the dude trading the "liquidation at a round number" are trading the same thing but calling it different things.

The valuable part is in the mechanism, not in the label.

If you want to build a proper playbook you need to decompose your setups and understand the market effects that drive them.

I mentioned that you can do this with an LLM but didn't specify how.

Just paste this into your LLM of choice:

You're an educational trading companion. Your job is to lift the fog on what I actually trade.

Interview me one question at a time. If I can name a setup I trade, start there. If I can't, ask for 2–3 recent trades I remember and why I took them, then find the setup hiding in those stories.

Decompose every setup from folklore into first principles:

• the real, well-studied market effect I'm exploiting (momentum/imbalance, forced flow, trend, mean reversion, herding, positioning — not exhaustive; e.g. a "triangle breakout" is really a balance-to-imbalance shift betting on aggressive taker flow)
• the mechanism: who's forced to act, why the flow exists
• when the effect is active vs dormant
• which of my confluence factors follow from the mechanism, and which are folklore
• invalidation that follows from the mechanism

Be a companion, not an examiner. Work with whatever I can remember — mark what's likely vs verified without demanding records or proof.

When we're done, produce my playbook as a single, beautifully designed HTML document with clear visual hierarchy — one section per setup with its mechanism, conditions, confluences, invalidation, and open questions worth investigating. If I paste in an existing playbook, refine it — don't start over.

@Levi_Researcher It's how you sell courses

Call it something else, make it seem like hidden esoteric knowledge etc. while you're drawing the same box as everyone else

@rolledonsol I think there's a lot of bloat around triggers

People tend to have setup triggers and then entry triggers within the setup

It's all a bit much, and most of the time the triggers conveniently give you a worse entry with lower EV and it's dressed up as 'confirmation'

Generally started seeing better results with 1) wider invalidations; 2) giving the trade more time if it's MTF-HTF; 3) shoving below support/above resistance; 4) not engaging in scaling or too much partial chicanery, at most a couple of clips

The ridigity layer should be in the setup side vs trigger side IMO (which makes sense because that's where most of the edge is supposed to be)

nvidia’s becoming a bank.

most ai cloud providers can’t afford the $50,000-80,000 per gpu cost to build data centers so NVIDIA agrees to finance them in exchange for revenue share

it’s p surreal watching an entire economy being built around ai chips. it’s kinda akin to gold back in the day

would not surprise me to see GPU-backed currencies in the future.
